texts.label: text
texts.orig: We have developed the simultaneous membrane distillation -crystallization (SMDC) hybrid desalination technology for the concurrent productions of pure water and salt crystal from the saturated brine solutions. The effects of feed temperature variation from 40 1 C to 70 1 C on the SMDC performance in terms of membrane fl ux and kinetics of NaCl crystallization have been investigated. Increasing feed temperature increases membrane fl ux but the fl ux declines rapidly with time at higher feed temperatures (60 1 C and 70 1 C) due to the occurrences of membrane scaling and wetting facilitated by salt oversaturation at the boundary layer. In order to prevent salt oversaturation, we have calculated the critical fl uxes at different Reynolds numbers and crystallizer temperatures. For instance, the critical fl uxes when the feed temperature is 70 1 C increase from 5 kg m -2 h -1 to 20 kg m -2 h -1 for the laminar and turbulent fl ows, respectively. By keeping the membrane fl ux lower than the critical fl ux, a stable membrane performance during a continuous SMDC operation over the period of 5000 min has been achieved. Increasing feed temperature also increases the yield of NaCl crystals from 7.5 kg per m 3 solution to 34 kg per m 3 for feed temperatures of 40 1 C and 70 1 C after 200 min operation, respectively. However, the average crystal sizes decrease from 87.40 m m to 48.82 m m with increasing feed temperatures from 40 1 C to 70 1 C due to a higher nucleation rate at a higher degree of supersaturation. Regardless of the feed temperature, the NaCl crystals are in a uniform cubical shape with the coef fi cient of variations which are in the range of 30 -38% that implies a narrow dispersion.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Comparing to other technologies, reverse osmosis (RO) has become the most dominant process in the desalination area (Global water intelligence and water desalination report, 2010). In the RO process, salty water is pressurized through a semipermeable membrane which allows selective permeation of water but rejects most of the dissolved ions. Due to the nature of the driving force, the speci fi c energy consumption (SEC) of RO increases greatly with increasing total water recovery (TWR) (Shaffer et al., 2012). This limits fresh water extraction from salty water with typically, the optimum TWR of a seawater RO plant is around 45 -60% (Drioli et al., 2006). As a consequence, a large amount of brine is produced as a by-product. At earlier date, the concentrated brine with a higher salinity of 65,000 -85,000 mg L -1 was normally disposed to the lakes, rivers or oceans (Lattemann and Höpner, 2008). Though this practice is deemed to be the least expensive option, its continuous practice will de fi nitely damage the marine ecosystem (Latorre, 2005). One way to mitigate the negative impact of desalination on the environment is by realizing the concept of zero liquid discharge. This can be accomplished by inventing a new desalination technology or combining the well established processes into a hybrid desalination technology. From the economical point of view, implementation of the hybrid desalination technology may be more advantageous because the additional processes can be directly integrated into the existing facilities. Moreover, by integrating different desalination processes, the operation and separation limitations which are encountered in the single desalination process might be overcome (Suk and Matsuura, 2006).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane distillation (MD) is a thermally driven desalination process in which a porous hydrophobic membrane is employed as a physical barrier between the hot feed, where the volatile compounds are evaporated, and the distillate side, where the diffused vapors are condensed (Bodell, 1963; Gryta, 2006; Khayet and Matsuura, 2001; Song et al., 2007; Teoh and Chung, 2009; Wang et al., 2009). The liquid is evaporated at the liquid -vapor interface on the membrane pore mouth and the vapor diffuses through the membrane pore because of the vapor pressure gradient across the membrane. Therefore, the permeation fl ux in MD is less sensitive to the change of solution concentrations as compared to pressure-driven desalination processes (Shaffer et al., 2012; Wang and Chung, 2012). As a result, the water extraction in the MD process is only limited by the maximum solute solubility provided that the membranes are not wetted and the operating pressures are lower than the liquid entry pressure (LEP). Once the feed solution has a concentration greater than the maximum solute solubility, scaling and wetting occur and these will result in the deteriorations of membrane fl ux and separation performance.

The liquid is evaporated at the liquid -vapor interface on the membrane pore mouth and the vapor diffuses through the membrane pore because of the vapor pressure gradient across the membrane. Therefore, the permeation fl ux in MD is less sensitive to the change of solution concentrations as compared to pressure-driven desalination processes (Shaffer et al., 2012; Wang and Chung, 2012). As a result, the water extraction in the MD process is only limited by the maximum solute solubility provided that the membranes are not wetted and the operating pressures are lower than the liquid entry pressure (LEP). Once the feed solution has a concentration greater than the maximum solute solubility, scaling and wetting occur and these will result in the deteriorations of membrane fl ux and separation performance.

texts.orig: In this context, MD integration with a crystallizer (MDC) will be able to surpass the saturation limit as when the solution reaches the saturation state, the saturated solute is crystallized out from the solution (Edwie and Chung, 2012; Gryta, 2002; Ji et al., 2010). Thus, the solute concentration is kept undersaturated throughout the operation and a high TWR ranging from 90 -95% for MDC operated on the RO brine has been reported (Ji et al., 2010; Tun and Groth, 2011). Therefore, the application of MDC not only can eliminate the costs for treatment and disposal of concentrated brine but can also recover valuable compounds from the brine. One of the drawbacks of the MDC process is the high SEC which is a typical issue for thermal desalination processes. At moderate temperatures (40 -50 1 C), the MDC operation requires a SEC of 15 -20 kWh per m 3 in which the thermal energy constitutes the largest portion (Macedonio et al., 2007). Nevertheless, the SEC of the MDC process can be reduced, for instance by integrating with solar energy, geothermal energy or other low-grade heat sources in the heating system (Bouguecha and Dhahbi, 2003; Charcosset, 2009; Hogan et al., 1991) while using the cold energy from the regasi fi cation process of lique fi ed natural gas (LNG) in the cooling system (Wang and Chung, 2012).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As MDC operates with saturated solutions, the operation is more challenging as compared to the MD process. As a combination of mass and heat transfer processes, MD suffers from both concentration and temperature polarizations resulting in a higher concentration with a lower temperature at the boundary membrane layer with respect to the bulk condition (Khayet and Matsuura, 2011). These polarizations not only suppress the driving force for the transport of water vapor across the membrane but also facilitate the nucleation and growth of inorganic salts that have positive solubility coef fi cients such as sodium chloride (NaCl) near the membrane surface (Tun et al., 2005). As a result, the deposition of salt crystals on membrane surface and inside membrane pores which is referred as inorganic scaling narrows the liquid -vapor interface and blocks the diffusion pathway of water vapor (Yun et al., 2006; Gryta, 2008). In addition to suppressing the permeation fl ux, the occurrence of inorganic scaling also causes membrane wetting in some cases (Gryta, 2007). Membrane wetting happens when the growth of salt crystals in the direction of water vapor shifts the liquid -vapor interface deeper into the membrane cross-section. Because of the stagnant fl uid movement inside the membrane pores, membrane wetting intensi fi es polarizations which in turn aggravate the nucleation and growth of salt crystals. Eventually, the distillate contamination is unavoidable once the gaseous gap between the salt solution and distillate is eliminated.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It is obvious that mitigation of polarization phenomena is an important issue in the application of MDC. Several methods have been suggested to overcome polarizations for instance by increasing fl ow velocity, applying gas bubbling, fabricating modules with a cross- fl ow con fi guration and installing turbulence promoter, baf fl e or spacer (Chen et al., 2013; Gryta, 2002; Li and Sirkar, 2004; Teoh et al., 2008). However, membrane design still plays an important role in MDC because it is impractical to completely eliminate polarizations. It is interesting to note that commercial membranes prepared from either polypropylene (PP) (Curcio et al., 2001; Gryta, 2002; Ji et al., 2010; Mariah et al., 2006) or polyvinylidene fl uoride (PVDF) (Tun and Groth, 2011; Tun et al., 2005; Yun et al., 2006) were employed in almost all MDC literatures. These commercial membranes were initially fabricated for the micro fi ltration (MF) process and therefore, the morphologies and transport properties of these membranes may not be suitable for the MDC process. Furthermore, the post spinning modifi cations of the membrane physicochemical properties are rather limited.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order to overcome such limitations, we fabricated PVDF hollow fi ber membranes with different con fi gurations and investigated their performance during the MD operation with highly concentrated salt solutions in our previous study (Edwie and Chung, 2012). We found that a thinner membrane with a smaller pore size and a cellular mixed-matrix structure underneath the membrane surface had a higher wetting resistance as compared to a thicker membrane with a larger pore size and a globular morphology. The reason is because a membrane with a smaller pore size has a higher liquid entry pressure while a cellular mixedmatrix structure increases tortuosity. Upon achieving saturation, the MD operation was terminated and NaCl was successfully recovered from the saturated brine by employing a cooling crystallizer operated under a batch-mode. However, as the MD and the crystallizer units were not operated simultaneously in our previous study, the salty water concentration increased with increasing operation time and hence, the productions of fresh water and salt were constrained by the equilibrium solubility.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Moreover, the stability of membrane performance could only be investigated for a short period of time as the MD operation had to be terminated before the salty water was oversaturated. Therefore, in this study, we aim to investigate the performance of our cellular mixed-matrix membrane which previously exhibited the highest wetting resistance in the newly proposed hybrid desalination system consisting of a MD and a crystallizer simultaneously. The effects of feed temperature on membrane permeation fl ux, distillate purity, scaling formation as well as kinetics of the nucleation and growth of NaCl crystals will be investigated. Although the effects of feed temperature on MD performance have been extensively studied (El-Bourawi et al., 2006), its effects on simultaneous membrane distillation -crystallization (SMDC) performance are rarely investigated. Furthermore, by modeling the heat and mass transfer processes, the critical fl uxes are estimated at different operating conditions; namely, feed temperature, Reynolds number and crystallizer temperature. The critical fl ux will serve as a guideline for membrane selection for the SMDC process. To avoid confusion, the term SMDC will henceforth be used to refer to a simultaneous integration of MD with a cooling crystallizer.

texts.orig: Commercial PVDF HSV 900 and cloisite 15 A clay particles were purchased from Arkema Inc. and Southern Clay Products, respectively. The cloisite 15 A clay particles were chosen as an additive because of its hydrophobicity and reinforcement effect on membrane mechanical properties (Edwie and Chung, 2012). PVDF powders and clay particles were dried at 60 1 C overnight under vacuum before using. N -methyl pyrrolidone (NMP) as a solvent, methanol (MeOH) as a non-solvent, and ethylene glycol (EG) as a pore-forming additive were supplied by Merck. NaCl was purchased from Merck and the deionized (DI) water was produced by a Milli-Q unit from Millipore. All of the chemicals were of reagent grade and used as received.

NaCl was purchased from Merck and the deionized (DI) water was produced by a Milli-Q unit from Millipore. All of the chemicals were of reagent grade and used as received.

texts.text: 2.2. Preparation of hollow fiber membrane

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The polymer solution was prepared by dispersing the clay particles in a mixture of NMP and EG under a vigorous stirring. Afterwards, the PVDF polymer was slowly added and the polymer solution was heated up to 60 1 C and continuously stirred until a homogeneous solution was attained. The hollow fi ber membrane was fabricated using a dry-jet wet phase inversion process (Edwie et al., 2012; Ong and Chung, 2012). Afterwards, the as-spun membrane was transferred into water for solvent exchange. After three days of solvent exchange, the membrane was freeze dried for performance tests and further characterizations. The detailed dope composition and spinning parameters are summarized in Table 1A. The dope composition and spinning parameters were selected based on our previous study (Edwie and Chung, 2012) where we found that the single-layer membrane spun under this particular spinning condition has a cellular mixed-matrix structure with signi fi cantly improved wetting resistance. Since the stability of membrane performance could only be investigated for a short period of time in our previous work due to the separate operations of MD and crystallizer units. The investigation of this membrane performance during a prolonged operation with the saturated solution is possible and important in this work as the solution concentration can be kept undersaturated in the newly proposed SMDC process.

texts.text: 2.4. SMDC experiments

texts.label: text
texts.orig: SMDC experiments were carried out using a laboratory scale setup shown in Fig. 2. Membrane modules were prepared by assembling 10 fi bers inside a 3/8 ' polypropylene housing and the effective module length ( Lm ) was about 10 cm. For all SMDC experiments, the crystallizer temperature was fi xed at 25 7 0.1 1 C and the feed solution was circulated from the crystallizer to the membrane module. The feed solution was prepared from 26.4 wt% NaCl (saturated NaCl concentration at 25 1 C) with the solution mass fi xed at 1000 g for each experiment. The feed solution was heated up from 25 7 0.1 1 C until 40 7 0.1 1 C and 70 7 0.1 1 C to increase the solubility of NaCl and therefore, preventing oversaturation in the module. Even though the solution temperature has a minor effect on the NaCl solubility, heating the salt solution is essential in the SMDC process due to the fact that the salt solution leaves the crystallizer at the saturated state with respect to the crystallizer operating temperature. Increasing solution temperature slightly increases the NaCl solubility and this lowers the saturation state of the solution before entering the membrane module where water evaporation occurs and the salt solution leaving the membrane module is still undersaturated. The salt solution was circulated through the shell side of the module with a fl ow velocity of 0.5 m s -1 while the retentate solution with a higher salinity was returned back to the crystallizer in which it was cooled down and became oversaturated. As a result, the supersaturated NaCl ions were crystallized from the solution until its concentration was reduced to 26.4 wt%. The NaCl concentration was determined by analyzing the solution density using a DMA 35 portable density meter from Anton Paar. The DI water employed as the circulating distillate was cooled down to 17 7 0.1 1 C and fl owed co-currently through the lumen side of the module with a fl ow velocity of 1 m s -1 . The increment of the distillate weight ( Mw ) was recorded by a beam balance (TB-2002) from Denver Instrument with an accuracy of 0.01 g. Any distillate contamination was monitored by evaluating the ionic conductivity of the distillate solution using a Lab 960 conductivity meter from

texts.orig: Two approaches were adopted to determine the yield of NaCl crystals at different feed temperatures. Firstly, the NaCl yield was calculated from the NaCl mass balance by assuming a total rejection of NaCl and the water loss from the feed solution was solely caused by the transport of water vapor across the membrane. Secondly, the calculated mass of NaCl crystals was veri fi ed by withdrawing all crystallized salts from the crystallizer at a predetermined residence time. The salt crystals were then fi ltered with a 0.2 m m polyethersulfone (PES) membrane fi lter from Nalgene and the total crystal mass was determined by a balance. The residence time was defi ned as the time allocated for the crystals to nucleate and grow in the crystallizer before the crystals were harvested. Unless otherwise stated, the residence time was fi xed at 60 min for all SMDC experiments.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1. Effects of feed temperature on performance of SMDC
texts.text: 3.1. Effects of feed temperature on performance of SMDC

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Feed temperature is an important parameter in the MD operation that affects membrane performance and thermal ef fi ciency. Increasing feed temperature increases rate of water evaporation because water molecules have a greater energy at an elevated temperature and the subsequent diffusion of water vapor is also enhanced because of the exponential dependence of vapor pressure on temperature. Consequently, the latent heat which is related to the phase change of water increases exponentially while the conduction heat increases linearly with increasing feed temperature. Thus, it is preferable to operate MD at a higher feed temperature to maximize the thermal ef fi ciency (Teoh and Chung, 2009; Wang et al., 2009). Fig. 3 plots evolution of the vapor permeation fl ux during the SMDC operation at different feed temperatures which shows a similar relation of vapor fl ux and temperature.
texts.text: Feed temperature is an important parameter in the MD operation that affects membrane performance and thermal ef fi ciency. Increasing feed temperature increases rate of water evaporation because water molecules have a greater energy at an elevated temperature and the subsequent diffusion of water vapor is also enhanced because of the exponential dependence of vapor pressure on temperature. Consequently, the latent heat which is related to the phase change of water increases exponentially while the conduction heat increases linearly with increasing feed temperature. Thus, it is preferable to operate MD at a higher feed temperature to maximize the thermal ef fi ciency (Teoh and Chung, 2009; Wang et al., 2009). Fig. 3 plots evolution of the vapor permeation fl ux during the SMDC operation at different feed temperatures which shows a similar relation of vapor fl ux and temperature.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As mentioned previously, the feed concentration to the MD module is constant in the SMDC process as the water evaporation and diffusion across the membrane is offset by the NaCl crystallization. Thus, the membrane fl ux is expected to be stable over the entire SMDC operation as other operating parameters are unchanged. However, Fig. 3 clearly shows that the vapor permeation fl uxes are only stable when the feed temperature is lower than 50 1 C while increasing the temperature to 60 1 C and 70 1 C causes a gradual reduction of membrane fl ux. The reason is because the feed temperature also governs the scaling formation in the SMDC process. NaCl has a positive solubility coef fi cient which means the salt solubility increases with increasing solution temperature. Moreover, as shown in Table 2, the reduction of the feed solution viscosity with increasing temperature raises the heat transfer coef fi cient at the feed boundary layer that increases the convective heat transfer from the bulk to the membrane surface. These retard scaling formation on the membrane.

The intensi fi cations of both concentration and temperature polarizations may cause oversaturation of the concentrated feed solution and hence, triggering salt crystallization in the membrane module. Table 2 shows that the concentration adjacent to the membrane surface ( Cf,m ) is always higher than the saturated concentration ( Csat ) and the deviation becomes larger with increasing feed temperature. Salt crystallization is accelerated at a higher saturation (Mullin, 2001). The subsequent deposition of salt crystals on the membrane surface (surface scaling) narrows the liquid -vapor interface and hence, causes a rapid decline of membrane fl ux at a higher feed temperature. Fig. 3 veri fi es this phenomenon for the membrane operated at 70 1 C, the fl ux reduction occurs immediately at the beginning of the experiment whereas for the membrane operated at 60 1 C, the fl ux is initially stable for the fi rst 60 min and gradually declines thereafter.

texts.orig: The internal membrane scaling also causes the transformation of membrane morphology as shown in Fig. 4A -C. It is obvious that when the feed temperature is 50 1 C, the membrane morphology is indifference from the as-spun membrane (displayed in Fig. 1). However, operating at higher feed temperatures (60 1 C and 70 1 C) transforms the membrane outer surface into a wavy structure and the morphology beneath the outer surface is also altered into a loose matrix with large cavities. The nucleation and growth of salt crystals inside the membrane matrices are restrained by the sizes of membrane cavities. However, due to the elasticity characteristic of the PVDF membrane as summarized in Table 1B, the growing crystals may stretch the membrane matrices and enlarge the cavities as displayed in Fig. 4B. The uneven crystal growth due to the variation of membrane pore sizes and correspondingly, the rates of water evaporation and diffusion may increase membrane surface roughness as shown in Fig. 4A. The structural change is more pronounced for membranes operated at higher feed temperatures due to the accelerated nucleation and growth of salt crystals at a higher saturation.

texts.orig: Other than yield, the formation of crystals with a desired crystal size distribution (CSD) is also an important aspect in any crystallizer operation. Either the crystals are the fi nal product or still require further treatments; the crystals need to be produced in a certain dimension and with a narrow CSD (Curcio et al., 2001). The crystals are formed through the nucleation and growth processes of the supersaturated solutes. During the crystallization process, both mechanisms occur simultaneously in a competitive manner. Therefore, understanding the effects of the SMDC operating parameters on the kinetics of crystal nucleation and growth are essential to tailor the fi nal CSD.

texts.orig: Solutes can only form a stable nucleus when the free energy of the nucleus is lower than the dissolved solutes. There are two types of energy change governing the homogeneous nucleation: (1) surface free energy because of the addition of a new solute molecule on the nucleus and (2) volume free energy because of the change of the nucleus size upon addition of a new solute molecule (De Yoreo and Vekilov, 2003). The surface free energy is a positive term and is dominant for a small nucleus which has abundant molecules on the surface. Therefore, the addition of one molecule to a small nucleus increases the system free energy and this unstable nucleus is most likely re-dissolved to reduce the system free energy. On the other hand, the volume free energy is a negative term and is dominant for a big nucleus. In this case, the addition of one molecule to a big nucleus reduces the system

texts.orig: free energy. In between there exists a critical size at which the system free energy reduces for both cases of the nucleus dissolution and the nucleus growth. The nucleation is controlled by the critical size because a smaller critical size increases the probability for formation of a stable nucleus. The critical size is smaller for a solution which has a higher degree of supersaturation (Garside and Davey, 1980). Therefore, the crystallization at higher feed temperatures is dominated by nucleation that results in the formation of smaller size crystals. The nucleation and growth rates reported in Table 3 verify that the nucleation rate increases while the growth rate decreases with increasing feed temperature. These kinetics data of the NaCl crystallization are comparable with the literatures. Garside and Shah (1980) reported that the growth rates for most inorganic salts were in the order of 10 -8 ms -1 while Ji et al. (2010) reported that the nucleation rate for the NaCl crystal was in the order of 10 9 -10 11 crystals per m 3 of the crystallizing solution.

texts.orig: Furthermore, the effects of residence time on CSD are also investigated. Fig. 7 shows the evolution of CSD with increasing residence time when the feed temperature is fi xed at 50 1 C. The crystal size increases but at the expense of a broader size distribution with increasing residence time. The reason is because of the deviation of real residence time for different crystals as the crystal nucleation may occur at any time and hence, the time available for crystals to grow may vary. Moreover, with increasing number of crystals in the crystallizer, the probability for the secondary nucleation increases because of a higher chance for crystal collision (Fakatselis, 2002). On the other hand, Fig. 8 shows that the CSD is relatively constant with increasing batch number where each batch corresponds to 60 min of residence time. For all batches at which the feed temperature is fi xed at 40 1 C (inset of Fig. 8B -D) or when the feed temperature is varied (inset of Fig. 6A -D), the NaCl crystals are in a uniform cubical shape with the coef fi cient of variation which is in the range of 30 -38% that implies a narrow dispersion.

texts.orig: It is important in the SMDC operation to ensure the salt concentration near the membrane surface lower than the saturated concentration in order to prevent scaling and membrane wetting. The salt concentration near the membrane surface is governed by the membrane fl ux and the salt mass transfer coef fi cient (Eq. (24)). Therefore, through modeling of the heat and mass transfer processes described in Section 2.6, the critical fl ux at which the salt concentration near the membrane surface is equal to the saturated concentration can be estimated. Thus, it is recommended to choose a membrane with a fl ux lower than the critical fl ux to prevent salt oversaturation in the membrane module.

texts.orig: 500, the critical fl uxes are estimated to be around 5 kg m -2 h -1 and 2 kg m -2 h -1 for the feed temperatures of 70 1 C and 50 1 C, respectively. By increasing the Reynolds number to 2,600, the critical fl ux increases to almost 20 kg m -2 h -1 for the feed temperature of 70 1 C. A higher Reynolds number provides a better mixing and hence, increases the heat and mass transfer coef fi cients at the boundary layer. As a result, the membrane operated under a turbulent fl ow can tolerate a higher water evaporation rate as compared to that of a laminar fl ow. From the production point of view, it is preferable to operate the SMDC process under a turbulent fl ow as it can enhance the production rates of fresh water and salt crystals. However, the turbulent fl ow may increase the probability for the crystal collision and breakage (Drioli et al., 2006). Thus, it is recommended to operate SMDC under a laminar fl ow if the aims are to produce crystals with a uniform shape and a narrow size distribution.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The effects of feed temperature on the SMDC performance have been investigated. It has been found that increasing feed temperatures to 60 1 C or 70 1 C causes a rapid reduction on the membrane fl ux while stable fl uxes are obtained during SMDC operations at lower feed temperatures (40 1 C or 50 1 C). Modeling of heat and mass transfers reveals that a higher rate of water evaporation induces higher concentration and temperature polarizations generating oversaturation in the module. This oversaturation facilitates both scaling and membrane wetting that deteriorate the membrane fl ux by three different mechanisms: suppressing the driving force for the transport of water vapor, narrowing the liquid -vapor interface, and blocking the diffusion pathway of water vapor.
texts.text: The effects of feed temperature on the SMDC performance have been investigated. It has been found that increasing feed temperatures to 60 1 C or 70 1 C causes a rapid reduction on the membrane fl ux while stable fl uxes are obtained during SMDC operations at lower feed temperatures (40 1 C or 50 1 C). Modeling of heat and mass transfers reveals that a higher rate of water evaporation induces higher concentration and temperature polarizations generating oversaturation in the module. This oversaturation facilitates both scaling and membrane wetting that deteriorate the membrane fl ux by three different mechanisms: suppressing the driving force for the transport of water vapor, narrowing the liquid -vapor interface, and blocking the diffusion pathway of water vapor.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The critical fl ux at which the concentration near the membrane surface is equal to the saturated concentration has been calculated at different Reynolds numbers and crystallizer temperatures. It has been found that the critical fl ux increases with increasing Reynolds number because of a higher mixing while it decreases with increasing crystallizer temperature because of a higher concentration of the solution entering the membrane module. Therefore, the prominent parameter to establish a steady SMDC performance is by ensuring that the membrane fl ux is always lower than the critical fl ux in order to prevent oversaturation. By ful fi lling this criterion, a stable membrane performance during a continuous SMDC operation over the period of 5000 min has been achieved.
texts.text: The critical fl ux at which the concentration near the membrane surface is equal to the saturated concentration has been calculated at different Reynolds numbers and crystallizer temperatures. It has been found that the critical fl ux increases with increasing Reynolds number because of a higher mixing while it decreases with increasing crystallizer temperature because of a higher concentration of the solution entering the membrane module. Therefore, the prominent parameter to establish a steady SMDC performance is by ensuring that the membrane fl ux is always lower than the critical fl ux in order to prevent oversaturation. By ful fi lling this criterion, a stable membrane performance during a continuous SMDC operation over the period of 5000 min has been achieved.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The effects of feed temperature on the NaCl yield and the kinetic of NaCl crystallization have also been investigated. Increasing feed temperature increases the NaCl yield because of a higher rate of water evaporation. Moreover, the average crystal size decreases with increasing feed temperature which implies the increasing dominance of nucleation over growth at a higher feed temperature. This can be attributed to the reduction of nucleus critical size at a higher supersaturation resulting in the formation of a large number of salt crystals with smaller sizes. The calculated nucleation and growth rates are in agreement with the experimental observation.
